title: 10 best herbal teas for gerd

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Aloe vera's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as aloin and aloe-emodin, can reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach lining, alleviating symptoms of GERD.

Soften mucous membranes property: The polysaccharides present in aloe vera can help soften and soothe the mucus membranes in the esophagus and stomach, reducing irritation and discomfort caused by GERD.

Gastroprotective properties: Aloe vera contains compounds that have been shown to have gastroprotective effects, including the ability to reduce acid production and prevent damage to the mucous lining of the stomach and esophagus, thus helping to alleviate symptoms of GERD.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Aloe vera, also known as aloe, teas helps with GERD because of its anti-inflammatory properties and soothing effects on the digestive system. The gel extracted from the aloe plant contains compounds like aloin and aloe-emodin, which have been shown to reduce inflammation and ease symptoms of acid reflux. By calming the esophagus and reducing acid production, Aloe vera tea can provide relief from heartburn, bloating, and discomfort associated with GERD, promoting a healthier digestive system. Regular consumption may also help prevent future episodes.
remedy_constituents: Aloin: Aloein helps with GERD by reducing inflammation in the esophagus and stomach, thereby alleviating symptoms such as heartburn and acid reflux.

Phenolic compounds (including Catechins and Chlorogenic acid): Phenolic compounds help with GERD by inhibiting the growth of Helicobacter pylori bacteria, which can contribute to stomach acid production and worsen GERD symptoms.
Saponins: Saponins help with GERD by forming a protective barrier on the mucous membranes of the esophagus and stomach, which can help soothe and protect the tissues from acid damage.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Aloe vera leaves are the most commonly used part for making teas due to their high concentration of aloe vera's soothing and anti-inflammatory properties.
Stems: Aloe vera stems contain a gel-like substance that can be extracted and used in teas to help alleviate heartburn and digestive issues associated with GERD.
Gel from Leaves: However, it is more accurate to refer to the gel extracted from the leaves rather than the leaf itself.
remedy_recipe: Gather 1-2 cups of aloe vera gel and 1 cup of water for making the tea.
Combine the aloe vera gel and water in a saucepan and gently heat it for 10 minutes.
Remove the saucepan from heat and let it steep for 15-20 minutes to allow the flavors to combine.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve to remove any solids and impurities.
Store the herbal aloe vera tea in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Ginger's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as gingerol and shogaol, help reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach, which can alleviate symptoms of GERD.

Carminative effects: The volatile oils present in ginger tea have carminative properties, which means they help to expel gas from the digestive tract, reducing pressure on the LES (lower esophageal sphincter) and minimizing acid reflux.

Antacid properties: Ginger's ability to increase saliva production helps neutralize stomach acid, while its antispasmodic properties can relax the muscles in the esophagus and prevent spasms that can exacerbate GERD symptoms.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger, teas helps with GERD because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties and ability to relax the muscles in the digestive tract. The active compounds in ginger, such as gingerols and shogaols, help to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms of acid reflux, including heartburn and bloating. Additionally, ginger's antispasmodic properties help to calm the lower esophageal sphincter, preventing stomach acid from flowing back up into the esophagus and providing relief from GERD symptoms.
remedy_constituents: Gingerols: These are sesquiterpene compounds that help alleviate GERD symptoms by reducing inflammation and relaxing the lower esophageal sphincter, thereby preventing acid reflux.
Shogaols: These compounds, derived from gingerols, have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help soothe the esophagus and reduce inflammation associated with GERD.

Paradol: This is a phenolic compound found in Zingiber officinale that has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which help reduce inflammation and prevent acid reflux in the esophagus.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: The rhizomes of ginger are commonly used to make teas due to their high concentration of gingerols and shogaols, which have anti-inflammatory properties that help alleviate GERD symptoms.
Roots: The roots of ginger, being the primary part used in most ginger preparations, are used to make teas that help soothe the digestive system and reduce inflammation associated with GERD.
Leaves: Although less commonly used, the leaves of ginger are occasionally used to make teas that may help with digestion and alleviate GERD symptoms due to their mild anti-inflammatory properties.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1-2 inches of fresh zingiber officinale rhizomes for optimal flavor and aroma.
Wash the rhizomes under cold running water to remove dirt and impurities gently.
Chop the rhizomes into small pieces using a sharp knife to release the active compounds quickly.
Steep 1 teaspoon of chopped rhizome in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes patiently.
Strain the tea through a fine mesh sieve to remove the solids completely.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Licorice tea contains flavonoids and glycyrrhizin, which have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach, alleviating symptoms of GERD.

Sothothic and anti-ulcer properties: Licorice tea has been shown to protect the mucous membranes of the esophagus and stomach from damage caused by acid reflux, reducing the risk of ulcers and erosion.
Gastroprotective compounds: The saponins present in licorice tea have been found to inhibit the production of gastric acid, reducing the severity of GERD symptoms such as heartburn and bloating.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra, also known as licorice, teas helps with GERD because of its anti-inflammatory properties and ability to soothe the digestive tract. The root of the licorice plant contains glycyrrhizin, a compound that has been shown to reduce inflammation and promote the healing of the esophagus and stomach lining. This can help alleviate symptoms of acid reflux, such as heartburn and discomfort, allowing individuals to experience relief from the painful and uncomfortable symptoms associated with GERD.
remedy_constituents: Licorice Glycosides (Glycyrrhizin): These compounds help with GERD by reducing inflammation and healing the mucous membranes in the esophagus and stomach, thereby reducing acid reflux.

Flavonoids (Quercetin): Quercetin, a flavonoid found in Glycyrrhiza glabra, has anti-inflammatory properties that help in reducing the inflammation of the esophagus and stomach lining, thereby alleviating GERD symptoms.

Terpenoids (Glycyrrhetinic Acid): Glycyrrhetinic acid, a terpenoid in Glycyrrhiza glabra, helps in reducing inflammation and stabilizing the stomach's pH balance, which in turn helps in managing GERD symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: Glycyrrhiza glabra roots are used to make teas for GERD due to their anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe the digestive system.
Leaves: The leaves of Glycyrrhiza glabra are used to make teas for GERD due to their ability to reduce inflammation and promote digestion.
Roots: Glycyrrhiza glabra roots are used to make teas for GERD due to their ability to reduce symptoms of acid reflux by stabilizing stomach acid levels.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh glycyrrhiza glabra roots from a trusted source or cultivate them in your garden to ensure freshness.
Wash the harvested roots thoroughly in cold running water to remove any dirt or debris for 2 minutes.
Chop the cleaned glycyrrhiza glabra roots into small pieces using a sharp knife to release the active compounds for 5 minutes.
Steep 1 teaspoon of chopped glycyrrhiza glabra roots in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release the flavors.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove any remaining root particles and enjoy.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Peppermint tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds like rosmarinic acid, which helps reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach lining, alleviating symptoms of GERD such as heartburn and discomfort.

Antispasmodic properties: The menthol content in peppermint tea relaxes the smooth muscle tissue in the digestive tract, reducing spasms and contractions that can exacerbate GERD symptoms like bloating, gas, and stomach pain.

Digestive soothing agents: Peppermint tea's soothing effects on the digestive system help to reduce irritability and inflammation in the esophagus, easing discomfort and heartburn associated with GERD, while also promoting healthy digestion and bowel function.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Mentha x piperita, also known as peppermint, teas helps with GERD because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties and ability to relax the muscles in the digestive tract. The menthol content in peppermint tea helps to reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach, alleviating symptoms such as heartburn and acid reflux. Additionally, peppermint tea's carminative properties help to ease digestion and reduce bloating, providing quick relief from GERD symptoms and promoting overall digestive health.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: Menthol helps with GERD by relaxing the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) and reducing inflammation, allowing for easier digestion and reduced acid reflux.
Rosmarinic acid: Rosmarinic acid helps with GERD by inhibiting the production of stomach acid and reducing inflammation in the esophagus, which can help alleviate symptoms of acid reflux.
Cineole (Eucalyptol): Cineole helps with GERD by reducing inflammation and relaxing the smooth muscle in the esophagus, allowing for easier digestion and reduced acid reflux.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Mentha x piperita leaves are used to make teas for GERD due to their anti-inflammatory properties and ability to soothe digestive issues.
Stems: The stems of Mentha x piperita are used in teas for GERD as they contain menthol, which helps to relax the muscles in the digestive tract and reduce symptoms.
Leaves: The leaves of Mentha x piperita are also used in teas for GERD as they have natural carminative properties, which help to reduce gas and alleviate discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh mentha x piperita leaves in the morning after the dew has evaporated, using clean scissors.
Wash the leaves thoroughly with filtered water, then pat them dry with paper towels gently.
Combine one teaspoon of dried mentha x piperita leaves with one cup of boiling water, steeping for five minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup, discarding the solids completely.
Serve the tea warm or chilled, garnished with a sprig of fresh mentha x piperita leaves for added flavor.

remedy_properties: Soothing mucilages: Slippery elm's mucilage property forms a protective barrier on the esophageal lining, reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with GERD.

Anti-inflammatory effects: The bioactive compounds in slippery elm tea have been shown to inhibit prostaglandins, which can contribute to inflammation and pain in the esophagus, thereby alleviating GERD symptoms.
Stabilizing gut motility: Slippery elm's fiber content helps regulate bowel function, preventing digestive backflow that can exacerbate GERD symptoms by reducing stomach acid reflux.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ulmus rubra, also known as slippery elm, teas helps with GERD because of its soothing and protective properties. The inner bark of the Ulmus rubra tree contains mucilages, which form a protective barrier on the mucous membranes in the esophagus and stomach. This barrier helps to reduce inflammation and prevent acid reflux, providing relief from GERD symptoms such as heartburn and discomfort. Additionally, slippery elm teas may also help to calm digestive issues, promoting a healthy and balanced gut.
remedy_constituents: Tannins: Tannins in Ulmus rubra tea may help alleviate Gerd symptoms by reducing inflammation and acid production in the stomach, providing a soothing effect.

Flavonoids (specifically Quercetin): Quercetin, a flavonoid found in Ulmus rubra, has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms of acid reflux, such as heartburn and bloating.

Phenolic acids (specifically Gallic acid): Gallic acid, a phenolic acid present in Ulmus rubra, has been shown to exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which may help protect the esophageal lining and reduce symptoms of Gerd.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of Ulmus rubra are used to make teas due to their anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe digestive issues associated with Gerd.
Stems: The stems of Ulmus rubra are used to make teas, as they contain compounds that may help reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms of Gerd.
Buds: The buds of Ulmus rubra are used to make teas, as they are rich in antioxidants and flavonoids that may help calm digestive issues and provide relief from Gerd symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 10-15 grams of ulmus rubra bark and roots in the fall or early winter season.
Dry the harvested ulmus rubra bark and roots in a warm place for 2-3 weeks.
Grind 5 grams of dried ulmus rubra bark and roots into a fine powder using a mortar.
Steep 1 teaspoon of the ground ulmus rubra powder in 250ml of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea and discard the solids then serve the herbal tea warm or at room temperature.

remedy_properties: Mucilage: Marshmallow tea contains a thick, protective mucilage that soothes and protects the mucous membranes lining the esophagus, stomach, and digestive tract, reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with GERD.

The mucilaginous compounds present in the plant coat the esophagus and stomach lining, preventing acid irritation and inflammation. Gallic acid: Gallic acid, a phenolic compound in Althaea officinalis, has anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce inflammation in the esophagus and alleviate GERD symptoms.
Glucuronic acid: Glucuronic acid, a component of mucilages, may help form a protective barrier in the esophagus, preventing acid reflux and reducing the severity of GERD symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: They are commonly used due to their high mucilage content, which can help soothe and protect the digestive tract.
Leaves: Leaves are used because they contain anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach.
Flowers: Flowers are used for their calming effects, which can help alleviate symptoms of GERD, such as heartburn and acid reflux.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2-3 tablespoons of dried root of this plant during the summer or early fall seasons.
Rinse the dried root in cold water to remove any impurities from its surface thoroughly.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried root in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes slowly.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve to remove any remaining root particles completely.
Allow the tea to cool before serving it to enjoy its soothing effects immediately.

remedy_constituents: Flavonoids: These plant compounds help alleviate GERD symptoms by reducing inflammation and improving the integrity of the gastric mucosa, thereby preventing acid reflux.

remedy_recipe: Gather 30-60 grams of dried ginkgo biloba leaves and 500ml of water for brewing.
Boil the water in a kettle or on a stovetop for 5-7 minutes to reach 95-100°c.
Steep 1-2 teaspoons of dried ginkgo biloba leaves in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup to remove solids.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 1/4 cup of dried foeniculum vulgare seeds or 2 tablespoons of fresh seeds from mature plants.
Rinse the seeds with cool water to remove any impurities or debris from harvesting.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried foeniculum vulgare seeds or 1 tablespoon of fresh seeds in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the seeds from the liquid.
Serve the foeniculum vulgare tea immediately and enjoy the digestive benefits and flavor of the tea.

Digestive enzyme stimulation: Dandelion root contains compounds that stimulate digestive enzymes, improving digestion and reducing symptoms of acid reflux such as bloating, gas, and abdominal discomfort.

Mucilage production enhancement: The mucilages present in dandelion tea help to soothe and protect the mucous membranes lining the stomach and esophagus, preventing irritation and inflammation caused by GERD.

Taraxasterol: This triterpenoid saponin in dandelion tea may help to relax the lower esophageal sphincter, reducing the frequency and severity of acid reflux episodes and providing relief from GERD symptoms.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers in the early morning when they are at their highest moisture content.
Dry the harvested taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers in a low-temperature oven at 100 degrees fahrenheit for 2 hours.
Steep 2 teaspoons of dried taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5 minutes.
Strain the taraxacum officinale tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove any loose particles.
Serve the taraxacum officinale tea warm or chilled within 30 minutes of preparation to preserve its nutritional value.

remedy_properties: Berberine: Berberine is a bioactive compound found in barberry that has natural antibacterial and antifungal properties, which can help reduce the growth of bacteria and fungi in the digestive tract that contribute to GERD symptoms.

Anti-inflammatory compounds: Barberry tea contains various anti-inflammatory compounds like berberastin, which can help reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach lining, alleviating symptoms such as heartburn, bloating, and discomfort associated with GERD.

Gastric soothing properties: Barberry tea has been shown to have a calming effect on the stomach mucosa, reducing irritation, inflammation, and acid reflux by coating and protecting the stomach lining, which can help alleviate GERD symptoms like burning sensations, regurgitation, and coughing.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Berberis vulgaris, also known as barberry, teas helps with GERD because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties and ability to soothe the digestive system. The tea contains berberine, a compound that has been shown to reduce inflammation in the esophagus and alleviate symptoms of acid reflux. Additionally, Berberis vulgaris tea has been found to have a relaxing effect on the lower esophageal sphincter, preventing stomach acid from flowing back up into the esophagus and causing discomfort. remedy_constituents: Berberine: Berberine, an alkaloid present in Berberis vulgaris, has been shown to inhibit the contraction of gastrointestinal smooth muscles, thereby reducing the symptoms of acid reflux and heartburn associated with GERD.

Vasakine: Vasakine, a phenolic compound found in Berberis vulgaris, exhibits anti-inflammatory properties that may help soothe the esophageal mucosa and reduce inflammation in the esophagus, a common issue in GERD patients.

Oleanolic acid: Oleanolic acid, a triterpenoid saponin in Berberis vulgaris, has been demonstrated to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that may help protect the esophagus from acid damage and reduce the severity of GERD symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are often used due to their high concentration of berberine, a compound that helps alleviate symptoms of GERD by reducing inflammation and relaxing the lower esophageal sphincter.
Leaves: The leaves are commonly used to make teas for GERD because they contain berberine and other alkaloids that help soothe the digestive tract and reduce acid production in the stomach.

Fruits: The fruits of Berberis vulgaris are used to make teas for GERD due to their high content of anthraquinones, which have anti-inflammatory properties that can help calm the digestive system and alleviate symptoms of GERD.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried berberis vulgaris roots and stems from a trusted source.
Dry the harvested berberis vulgaris parts in a low-temperature oven at 150 degrees fahrenheit for 2 hours.
Crush or grind 1-2 teaspoons of dried berberis vulgaris into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle.
Steep 1 teaspoon of ground berberis vulgaris in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a cup and discard the solids.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chamomile teas helps with GERD because its soothing properties can calm the digestive system, reducing inflammation and spasms in the esophageal lining. Antioxidant properties: The antioxidants present in chamomile tea help protect the mucous membranes of the esophagus from damage caused by chronic exposure to stomach acid, reducing the risk of inflammation and tissue damage.

Muscle relaxant effects: Chamomile tea's apigenin also has a calming effect on the muscles in the digestive tract, which can help relax the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) and reduce symptoms of GERD such as regurgitation and coughing.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried chamomile flowers according to manufacturer's instructions for recommended quantity.
Measure one teaspoon of dried chamomille flowers into a heat-resistant cup or infuser.
Boil freshly drawn water in a kettle and pour it over the chamomile flowers, allowing it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove any loose particles and retain the flavorful liquid.
Allow the tea to cool to room temperature before consuming one to two cups daily as needed to alleviate gerd symptoms.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Kansas coneflower teas helps with GERD because its active compounds, such as ursolic acid and triterpenoids, have been shown to reduce inflammation in the esophagus and alleviate symptoms of gastroesophageal reflux disease. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties help to soothe and protect the mucous membranes lining the esophagus, reducing the likelihood of acid reflux and heartburn. Antacid properties: The herb has been traditionally used as a natural antacid, helping to neutralize excess stomach acid and relieve symptoms of heartburn and acid reflux common in GERD patients.

Soften mucus and reduce inflammation in the esophagus: Kansas Coneflower's mucilage-rich composition can help soften and protect the lining of the esophagus, reducing inflammation and irritation caused by GERD.

remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried echinacea angustifolia root into a heat-resistant cup or teapot.
Boil one cup of filtered water and pour it over the herb in the cup or teapot.
Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes to release the active compounds and flavonoids.
Strain the tea into a separate cup using a tea strainer or piece of cheesecloth to remove the solids.
Drink the tea immediately, or let it cool to room temperature before storing it in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Turmeric teas helps with GERD because it contains curcumin, a powerful anti-inflammatory compound that reduces inflammation in the esophagus and stomach lining. This calming effect alleviates symptoms such as heartburn, acid reflux, and regurgitation. Additionally, turmeric's natural antimicrobial properties help to eliminate harmful bacteria and yeast from the digestive tract, reducing the growth of unwanted microorganisms that can exacerbate GERD symptoms. By soothing the digestive system and promoting a healthy gut environment, turmeric teas provide natural relief from GERD discomfort.
remedy_properties: Curcumin: Turmeric tea contains curcumin, a polyphenol compound that has potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, which helps reduce inflammation in the esophagus and alleviate GERD symptoms.

Caffeoylquinic acids (CQA): The CQAs present in turmeric tea have been shown to possess gastroprotective properties, including reducing gastric acid secretion and improving mucous barrier function, thereby providing relief from heartburn and acid reflux.

Flavonoids: Turmeric tea also contains flavonoids, which have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects that help protect the esophageal lining from damage caused by GERD, reducing the risk of complications such as esophagitis and esophageal cancer.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: The rhyzome, or underground stem, of turmeric contains high levels of curcuminoids, which are natural anti-inflammatory compounds that can help alleviate symptoms of acid reflux and heartburn.

Roots: Turmeric roots contain a unique combination of bioactive compounds, including gingerols and shogaols, which have been shown to reduce inflammation and alleviate digestive issues associated with GERD.
remedy_recipe: Gather one teaspoon of dried turmeric powder or two tablespoons of fresh turmeric root.
Boil one cup of filtered water in a teapot or kettle for 3-5 minutes.
Add the turmeric powder or fresh turmeric root to the boiling water and stir well.
Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes before straining and discarding the solids.
Serve the tea hot or warm, sweetened with honey if desired, within 15-20 minutes.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Mullein teas helps with GERD because it possesses anti-inflammatory properties that soothe and calm the esophageal lining, reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with acid reflux. The natural flavonoids and saponins present in mullein also help to relax the lower esophageal sphincter, allowing food to pass through the stomach more efficiently and reducing the likelihood of stomach contents flowing back up into the esophagus. This herbal tea has been traditionally used to alleviate symptoms of heartburn and indigestion, providing natural relief for those suffering from GERD.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Mullein tea's anti-inflammatory compounds help reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach lining, alleviating symptoms like heartburn and acid reflux associated with GERD.

Antacid and soothing properties: The mucilage present in mullein tea helps neutralize stomach acid and soothe the esophageal lining, providing rapid relief from heartburn, bloating, and discomfort caused by GERD.

Soothing and protecting mucous membranes: Mullein's anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties also help protect and heal the mucous membranes in the mouth, throat, and digestive tract, which can become irritated and damaged due to prolonged acid reflux.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the mullein plant are used to create a soothing and calming tea that can help alleviate GERD symptoms by reducing inflammation in the digestive tract.
Leaves: Mullein leaves have anti-inflammatory properties, which make them effective in reducing acid reflux and heartburn associated with GERD when consumed as a medicinal tea.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2 ounces of dried verbascum thapsus flowers from a trusted source for this medicinal tea blend.
Measure one teaspoon of the dried herb into a large cup or teapot.
Bring eight ounces of pure water to a boil in a kettle or on the stovetop and steep the herb for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into another container to remove any solids.
Let the tea cool to room temperature before storing it in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Plantain teas helps with GERD because its anti-inflammatory properties soothe the esophageal lining, reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with acid reflux. The natural astringent properties of plantain also help to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, which can contribute to symptoms of GERD such as bloating and burning sensations. Additionally, plantain's gentle antacid properties help to neutralize stomach acids, providing relief from heartburn and indigestion. This natural remedy has been used for centuries to alleviate GERD symptoms and promote digestive health.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory property: Plantain tea has anti-inflammatory compounds that reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach lining, which helps alleviate symptoms of acid reflux and heartburn.
Antacid property: The mucilage content in plantain tea forms a protective barrier on the mucous membranes, neutralizing stomach acid and providing relief from burning sensations associated with GERD.

Soothing and calming property: Plantain tea's soothing and calming effects help to reduce spasms and irritation in the esophagus and stomach, which can alleviate symptoms of GERD such as discomfort and chest pain.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in fiber and mucilage, plantain roots help soothe and calm digestive issues associated with Gerd.
Barks: The bittersweet flavor of plantain barks has been traditionally used to relax the muscles in the digestive tract, reducing symptoms of heartburn.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh plantago major leaves and flowers from your garden or local supplier.
Dry the harvested plant material in a warm dark place for 1-2 weeks.
Measure out 1 teaspoon of dried plantago major per 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse into the tea.
Strain and drink the tea immediately or store it in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Yarrow teas helps with GERD because it contains flavonoids, which have a calming effect on the esophageal muscles. This reduces inflammation and spasms that can lead to acid reflux and heartburn. Additionally, yarrow's antacid properties help neutralize stomach acid, providing relief from discomfort and pain. By soothing and protecting the mucous membranes in the digestive tract, yarrow tea helps to alleviate symptoms of GERD, promoting a sense of calm and comfort for those who suffer from this condition.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Yarrow tea's antispasmodic compounds help to relax the smooth muscle walls of the esophagus and stomach, reducing the frequency and severity of heartburn symptoms.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The polyphenols and flavonoids present in yarrow tea have anti-inflammatory effects, which can reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach lining, alleviating GERD discomfort.
Antacid properties: Yarrow tea's alkaline pH helps to neutralize stomach acid, providing relief from acid reflux symptoms by reducing the amount of acid that flows back up into the esophagus.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in compounds that help soothe stomach inflammation and reduce acid reflux symptoms.
Leaves: Containing flavonoids and phenolic acids that act as natural anti-inflammatory agents, helping to ease digestive discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried achillea millefolium flowers into a small infuser or tea ball.
Bring four cups of pure water to a boil in a medium saucepan and let it cool for five minutes.
Steep the achillea millefolium mixture in boiling water for 10-15 minutes depending on desired strength.
Strain the tea into a large cup or teapot using a strainer or cheesecloth to remove loose leaves.
Allow the tea to cool down and steep for an additional 30 minutes before drinking.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ceylon cinnamon teas helps with GERD because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties, which soothe the digestive tract and reduce inflammation in the esophagus. The tea's antimicrobial compounds also inhibit the growth of bacteria that can exacerbate acid reflux symptoms. Additionally, Ceylon cinnamon's warming properties help stimulate digestion and relieve spasms in the stomach and intestines, promoting a smoother flow of food and reducing discomfort associated with GERD.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant Properties: Ceylon cinnamon tea contains high levels of antioxidants, which help to neutralize free radicals and reduce inflammation in the esophagus, alleviating symptoms of acid reflux.

Anti-Inflammatory Properties: The active compound cinnamic aldehyde in Ceylon cinnamon has anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce inflammation in the esophageal lining, soothing heartburn and GERD symptoms.

Gastroprotective Properties: Ceylon cinnamon tea has been shown to exhibit gastroprotective effects by reducing gastric acid production, protecting against damage caused by stomach acid reflux, and promoting healing of the esophagus.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in bioactive compounds, roots are used to create a soothing tea that can help reduce inflammation and alleviate digestive discomfort associated with GERD.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzome, which is the underground stem of the Ceylon cinnamon plant, has been traditionally used to make teas that aid in digestion and relieve symptoms of heartburn and acid reflux.
remedy_recipe: Harvest cinnamomum verum bark fresh or dried in quantities of 2-3 tablespoons for every cup of boiling water.
Grind the harvested cinnamomum verum bark into a fine powder using a coffee grinder or mortar and pestle for 30 seconds.
Boil one cup of filtered water in a saucepan then reduce heat to low simmering for 10-15 minutes.
Add 2-3 tablespoons of ground cinnamomum verum powder to the boiling water and stir counterclockwise for 5 seconds.
Allow the tea to steep for 7-10 minutes before straining with a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup for consumption.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Red clover teas helps with GERD because it contains isoflavones, which have been shown to reduce inflammation in the esophagus and alleviate symptoms of gastroesophageal reflux disease. The flavonoids present in red clover also help to relax the lower esophageal sphincter, reducing acid reflux and heartburn episodes. Additionally, the anti-inflammatory properties of red clover may help to soothe and heal the esophageal lining, further alleviating GERD symptoms. Overall, red clover tea may provide a natural solution for managing GERD symptoms.
remedy_properties: Isoflavones: Isoflavones, specifically formononetin and biochanin A, have been shown to reduce inflammation in the esophagus and alleviate symptoms of acid reflux by increasing the production of mucin, a thick protective layer in the stomach lining.

Phenolic acids: The phenolic acids present in red clover tea, such as caffeic and ferulic acids, have antioxidant properties that help to neutralize free radicals and reduce oxidative stress in the esophagus, which can contribute to GERD symptoms.

Saponins: Saponins, a type of triterpenoid compound found in red clover, have anti-inflammatory and antacid properties that may help to soothe and protect the mucous membranes in the stomach and esophagus, reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with acid reflux.
remedy_parts: Roots: Red clover roots contain triterpenoid saponins, which can help soothe and protect the esophageal lining, reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with GERD.
Flowers: Red clover flowers possess anti-inflammatory compounds that may help reduce symptoms of GERD by calming irritated mucous membranes in the digestive tract.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh red clover flowers and leaves or purchase dried botanicals for this recipe.
Combine 1 tablespoon of dried red clover with 1 cup boiling water in a heat-resistant teapot or infuser.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes, allowing the herbs to release their medicinal properties and flavors into the liquid.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a large glass measuring cup or teapot.
Pour the tea into individual cups and let it cool to a comfortable drinking temperature before serving immediately.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to GERD that are treated with herbal teas are acid reflux, heartburn, and indigestion. Certain herbs such as ginger, peppermint, and chamomile have natural anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe the digestive tract and relieve symptoms of these conditions. Other herbs like licorice root and slippery elm also provide relief by coating the esophagus and reducing inflammation. Drinking herbal teas made from these ingredients can provide a natural and gentle way to manage these ailments.
intro_desc: Herbal teas for Gerd are natural remedies that help alleviate symptoms of gastroesophageal reflux disease by reducing inflammation, relaxing the muscles, and promoting digestion. The best herbal teas for Gerd include Zingiber officinale, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Foeniculum vulgare, Cinchona officinalis, Mentha x piperita, Lavandula angustifolia, Matricaria chamomilla, Gardenia jasminoides, Ginkgo biloba, and Aloe barbadensis. If left untreated, Gerd can lead to chronic coughing, difficulty swallowing, and even tooth erosion, severely impacting daily life, sleep, and overall well-being.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal teas that help with GERD is a blend of peppermint, ginger, and licorice root. Peppermint tea helps to relax the muscles in the digestive tract, reducing symptoms of heartburn and nausea. Ginger tea aids in digestion and reduces inflammation in the esophagus, while licorice root tea soothes and protects the mucous membranes, preventing acid reflux. Drinking a combination of these teas after meals can provide relief from GERD symptoms and promote overall digestive health.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by Journal of alternative and complementary medicine (New York, N.Y.), herbal teas have shown potential benefits in alleviating symptoms of Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). The study, which reviewed 22 studies including 9 animal studies and 13 human studies, found that various herbal medicines, such as Myrtus communis and Cydonia oblonga, have led to a marked reduction in GERD symptoms comparable to the commonly used medication omeprazole. Additionally, herbal preparations like rikkunshito and Tongjlang have shown therapeutic effects for non-erosive reflux disease (NERD) where proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) failed to show a promising effect, indicating that herbal teas may be a useful complementary approach in managing GERD.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale teas helps with GERD because it has natural anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the digestive tract and reduce inflammation in the esophagus. The active compound, gingerol, helps to relax the lower esophageal sphincter, preventing stomach acid from flowing back up into the esophagus. Additionally, ginger has been shown to improve digestion and reduce symptoms of nausea and heartburn, providing relief for individuals suffering from GERD. Its unique combination of properties makes it an effective natural remedy for managing GERD symptoms.
remedy_constituents: Shogaols: These compounds help alleviate GERD symptoms by reducing inflammation in the esophagus and stomach lining.
Gingerols: Gingerols assist in calming down acid reflux and reducing inflammation in the digestive tract, providing relief from GERD symptoms.

Terpenes (specifically, β-sesquiphellandrene): Terpenes, particularly β-sesquiphellandrene, have been shown to relax the lower esophageal sphincter, which can help prevent stomach acid from flowing back up into the esophagus and causing GERD symptoms.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: Used for their anti-inflammatory properties, which help alleviate GERD symptoms.
Roots: Employed for their carminative properties, which aid in digestion and reduce heartburn.
Leaves: Utilized for their soothing effects, which calm the digestive tract and alleviate GERD discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh ginger roots, approximately 2-3 inches in length, for optimal flavor and aroma, taking about 10 minutes.
Peel the roots using a vegetable peeler, removing any brown or damaged skin, taking about 5 minutes.
Slice the peeled ginger roots into thin rounds, about 1/8 inch thick, for easier infusion, taking about 5 minutes.
Combine 1-2 teaspoons of sliced ginger roots with 1 cup of boiling water in a teapot, steeping for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup and serve hot, garnished with a slice of fresh ginger for added flavor.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas helps with GERD because it has anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the digestive tract and reduce inflammation in the esophagus. Licorice root, the key component of Glycyrrhiza glabra, also has natural mucilage properties that form a protective barrier on the stomach lining, preventing acid from flowing up into the esophagus and causing heartburn. Additionally, Glycyrrhiza glabra teas may help reduce acid production and improve digestion, providing relief from GERD symptoms.
remedy_constituents: Saponins: Saponins help with GERD by reducing inflammation and protecting the mucous membranes in the esophagus and stomach, preventing acid reflux and heartburn.

Flavonoids (specifically, quercetin and isorhapontigenin): Flavonoids help with GERD by reducing inflammation and inhibiting the production of stomach acid, which can exacerbate symptoms of acid reflux and heartburn.

Glycyrrhizin: Glycyrrhizin helps with GERD by inhibiting the enzyme 11-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase, which converts cortisol to a more potent form that can increase stomach acid production, and also has anti-inflammatory properties to soothe the digestive tract.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are used due to their high glycyrrhizin content, which helps to reduce inflammation and acid production in the stomach.
Leaves: The leaves are used for their antispasmodic properties, which help to relax the muscles in the digestive tract and alleviate symptoms of GERD.
Barks: The barks are used due to their anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which help to soothe the digestive tract and reduce inflammation associated with GERD.
remedy_recipe: Measure out the dried root of glycyrrhiza glabra in a ratio of 1 tablespoon per 8 ounces of water.
Combine the measured root with 8 ounces of boiling water in a heat-resistant cup.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes or until the liquid reaches a comfortable temperature for drinking.
Strain the tea into a separate cup to remove any remaining root particles or debris.
Store any leftover tea in a sealed container in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

remedies.remedy_desc: Foeniculum vulgare teas helps with GERD because it possesses anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the esophageal lining, reducing inflammation and discomfort. The tea also contains carminative compounds that help alleviate bloating and gas, common triggers for acid reflux. Additionally, Foeniculum vulgare has been found to relax the lower esophageal sphincter, preventing stomach acid from flowing back up into the esophagus and providing relief from GERD symptoms. Its natural properties make it an effective herbal remedy for managing the condition.
remedy_constituents: Anethole: This terpene helps to relax the lower esophageal sphincter, reducing acid reflux and alleviating GERD symptoms.

Phenolic acids (e.g., ferulic acid and sinapic acid): These compounds have anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce inflammation in the esophagus and alleviate symptoms associated with GERD.
Fenchone: This terpene has a calming effect on the digestive system, reducing spasms and inflammation in the esophagus and alleviating symptoms of GERD, including heartburn and acid reflux.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves are the most commonly used part for tea due to their high concentration of volatile oils, which have carminative properties that help alleviate digestive discomfort.
Seeds: Fennel seeds are used to make tea due to their high content of anethole, a compound that has natural anti-inflammatory and anti-spasmodic properties, which can help soothe GERD symptoms.
Flowers: Fennel flowers are used to make tea due to their ability to calm digestive issues and reduce inflammation, making them a natural remedy for alleviating GERD symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Gather fresh foeniculum vulgare leaves and flowers in an amount of 2 teaspoons for every 8 oz of water.
Steep the gathered foeniculum vulgare in 8 oz of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release the flavors.
Strain the foeniculum vulgare mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove the solids.
Add honey to taste to the strained foeniculum vulgare tea for a sweet flavor.
Serve the foeniculum vulgare tea immediately and drink it warm for maximum benefits.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cinchona officinalis teas helps with GERD because it contains quinine, a natural alkaloid with anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe the digestive tract and reduce inflammation in the esophagus. The tea's bitter compounds also stimulate digestion, promoting the production of stomach acid and improving the clearance of food particles from the esophagus, reducing the likelihood of acid reflux and symptoms associated with GERD. Additionally, Cinchona officinalis may help relax the lower esophageal sphincter, preventing stomach acid from flowing back up into the esophagus.
remedy_constituents: Quinine: Helps with GERD by reducing inflammation in the esophagus and alleviating heartburn symptoms.
Quinovic acid glycoside (Quinovic acid): Aids in reducing symptoms of GERD by inhibiting the production of stomach acid, which in turn helps in reducing heartburn and acid reflux.
Quinic acid: Acts as a natural antacid, helping to neutralize stomach acid and alleviate symptoms of GERD such as heartburn and indigestion.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Alkaloids like quinine, which helps to reduce heartburn and alleviate symptoms of GERD.
Barks: Tannins and other compounds found in the bark help to reduce inflammation in the esophagus and alleviate heartburn.
Roots: Saponins present in the roots may help to soothe the digestive system and reduce inflammation in the esophagus.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried cinchona officinalis bark and clean it thoroughly under cold running water.
Dry the cleaned bark in a single layer at 50 to 60 degrees celsius for 2 hours.
Grind 2 to 3 grams of dried cinchona officinalis bark into fine powder using a spice grinder.
Steep 1 teaspoon of ground cinchona officinalis in 250 milliliters of boiling water for 10 to 15 minutes.
Strain the tea immediately and serve warm or allow it to cool before consumption.

remedies.remedy_desc: Mentha x piperita teas helps with GERD because of its natural antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties. The tea's peppermint oil content helps relax the lower esophageal sphincter, reducing symptoms of acid reflux and heartburn. Additionally, the tea's cooling effect on the digestive system soothes and calms the stomach lining, reducing inflammation and discomfort. This natural remedy can provide relief from the burning sensation and discomfort associated with GERD, offering a healthy and non-pharmacological approach to managing symptoms.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: Helps with GERD by providing a local anesthetic effect on the esophagus, reducing the sensation of heartburn and discomfort.
Menthone: Contributes to the anti-inflammatory and calming properties of the tea, which can help reduce inflammation and soothe the esophageal lining.
Rosmarinic Acid: Exhibits antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the esophagus, alleviating symptoms of GERD.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Used for their calming effect and ability to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract.
Stems: Used for their menthol content, which helps to relax the muscles in the digestive tract and reduce symptoms of GERD.
Leaves: (as the most common part) Used for their soothing and calming effects, which can help to reduce symptoms of heartburn and acid reflux.
remedy_recipe: Harvest the fresh leaves of mentha x piperita in the morning after the dew has evaporated.
Chop the freshly harvested leaves into small pieces using a sharp knife to release their oils.
Combine 1 tablespoon of chopped mentha x piperita leaves with 1 cup of boiling water in a teapot.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the leaves to infuse their flavors and oils.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup and serve immediately while hot.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia teas helps with GERD because its calming effects can soothe the digestive system, reducing inflammation and easing symptoms of acid reflux. The tea's antispasmodic properties also help to relax the muscles in the esophagus and stomach, preventing stomach acid from flowing back up into the esophagus. Additionally, the tea's ability to calm the nervous system can help to reduce stress, a common trigger for GERD symptoms. This can lead to improved digestion and reduced discomfort.
remedy_constituents: Linalool: This terpene has anti-inflammatory and relaxing properties, which can help reduce stomach acid production and alleviate symptoms of GERD.
Linalyl acetate: This terpene has a calming effect on the digestive system, which can help reduce stress-induced stomach acid production and alleviate symptoms of GERD.
Rosmarinic acid: This phenolic acid has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation and damage to the esophageal lining, alleviating symptoms of GERD.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are commonly used due to their calming and soothing properties, which can help alleviate the symptoms of GERD.
Flowers: Lavender flowers are often used in teas as they are known to have a relaxing effect on the digestive system, which can help ease GERD symptoms.
Stems: Some herbal teas may use the stems of Lavandula angustifolia as they contain compounds that can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, alleviating GERD symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20 to 30 fresh lavandula angustifolia flowers in the early morning to preserve their potency.
Rinse the flowers gently in cold water to remove dirt and debris from the buds.
Steep 1 tablespoon of dried or 2 tablespoons of fresh flowers in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to separate the liquid from the solids.
Allow the tea to cool completely before serving it in a cup or storing it in the refrigerator.

remedies.remedy_desc: Matricaria chamomilla teas helps with GERD because it has anti-inflammatory and soothing properties that can calm the digestive system. The flavonoids and terpenoids present in the tea have a natural ability to reduce inflammation and relax the muscles in the esophagus, which can help alleviate symptoms of acid reflux. Additionally, chamomile tea can also help to reduce stress and anxiety, which are common triggers for GERD episodes, promoting a sense of calm and relaxation in the body. This can help to alleviate symptoms and promote overall digestive health.
remedy_constituents: Apigenin: This flavonoid acts as an anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic agent, helping to soothe and relax the muscles in the digestive tract, thus reducing symptoms of GERD.
Luteolin: This flavonoid has been shown to have a protective effect on the esophageal mucosa, reducing inflammation and preventing damage caused by acid reflux.
Alpha-bisabolol: This sesquiterpene has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, helping to calm down the digestive system and reduce symptoms of GERD, such as heartburn and acid reflux.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Rich in apigenin and luteolin, which have anti-inflammatory and soothing effects on the digestive system.
Leaves: Contain flavonoids and terpenoids that help reduce inflammation and promote relaxation, alleviating GERD symptoms.
Seeds: Have a sedative effect, which can help calm the digestive system and reduce symptoms of GERD.
remedy_recipe: Gently harvest dried flowers of matricaria chamomilla from a clean and dry storage container.
Measure out 1 teaspoon of dried flowers for a standard serving size of tea.
Steep the dried flowers in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the solids.
Serve the tea hot or at room temperature within 30 minutes of preparation.

remedies.remedy_desc: Gardenia jasminoides teas helps with GERD because of its unique combination of antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties. The tea's active compounds, such as gardenoside and geniposide, have been shown to relax the lower esophageal sphincter, reducing symptoms of acid reflux and heartburn. Additionally, the tea's soothing effects on the digestive tract may help to calm inflammation and irritation, promoting a more balanced and healthy digestive system. This natural remedy can be a valuable adjunct to conventional treatments for GERD.
remedy_constituents: Flavonoids: Flavonoids in Gardenia jasminoides tea have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the esophagus and alleviate symptoms associated with GERD.
Phenolic acids: Phenolic acids in Gardenia jasminoides tea possess antioxidant properties, which can help protect the esophagus from oxidative damage and reduce the risk of acid reflux.

Triterpenoids: Triterpenoids in Gardenia jasminoides tea have been shown to have a relaxing effect on the lower esophageal sphincter, which can help prevent stomach acid from flowing back up into the esophagus and alleviate GERD symptoms.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Used to make tea for Gerd due to their soothing and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate digestive issues.
Flowers: Used to make tea for Gerd due to their calming and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce stomach acid and alleviate symptoms.
Buds: Used to make tea for Gerd due to their potential to help soothe digestive issues and reduce inflammation in the stomach.
remedy_recipe: Choose fresh gardenia jasminoides flowers in good condition for making herbal teas.
Harvest 2-3 teaspoons of fresh or dried flowers for a standard serving size.
Steep the flowers in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to extract flavors.
Strain the tea mixture to remove the flowers and discard the solids immediately.
Serve the gardenia jasminoides tea hot or chilled depending on your preference.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba teas helps with GERD because its flavonoids and terpenoids have anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the digestive tract and reduce inflammation in the esophagus. This can help alleviate symptoms of acid reflux such as heartburn and chest pain. Additionally, ginkgo biloba tea's antioxidant properties help protect the stomach lining from damage caused by excess acid, promoting a healthy balance of stomach acid and reducing symptoms of GERD. remedy_constituents: Flavonoids: They help with GERD by reducing inflammation and improving the integrity of the gastric mucosa, which can help alleviate symptoms of acid reflux.
Bilobalide: It helps with GERD by inhibiting the production of acid in the stomach and reducing the symptoms of heartburn and nausea associated with acid reflux.
Quercetin: It helps with GERD by acting as an anti-inflammatory agent, reducing the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes and cytokines that can exacerbate acid reflux symptoms.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The most commonly used part for Ginkgo biloba tea, as they contain flavonoids and terpenoids that help alleviate symptoms of GERD.
Seeds: Used to create a less common form of Ginkgo biloba tea, as they contain compounds that may help soothe digestive issues associated with GERD.

Leaves is the only one listed so I'm going to list the second most used part:  Barks: Less commonly used than leaves, but still used in some tea formulations, as the bark may contain compounds that aid in digestion and alleviate GERD symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 60 grams of ginkgo biloba leaves from mature trees when they are in full leaf during late spring.
Rinse the ginkgo biloba leaves with cold water to remove any dirt or debris.
Dry the ginkgo biloba leaves in a warm place for 2-3 days or using a food dehydrator for 6-8 hours.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried ginkgo biloba leaves in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea and serve it hot or allow it to cool before consuming it immediately.

remedies.remedy_desc: Aloe barbadensis teas helps with GERD because it has anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the digestive tract and reduce inflammation in the esophagus. The gel-like substance in aloe vera contains mucilages that coat the stomach lining, preventing acid reflux and reducing irritation. Additionally, aloe vera's anti-spasmodic properties help to relax the muscles in the digestive tract, reducing symptoms of acid reflux and heartburn associated with GERD, providing relief and promoting a healthier digestive system.
remedy_constituents: Anthraquinones: These compounds help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate symptoms of GERD by calming the stomach lining and preventing acid reflux.

Flavonoids: Flavonoids, such as kaempferol and quercetin, have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help protect the esophageal mucosa from acid damage and reduce inflammation in the digestive system.

Phenolic acids: Phenolic acids, including vanillic acid and ferulic acid, exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help protect the stomach lining from acid damage, reduce inflammation, and alleviate symptoms of GERD.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Aloe vera leaves are used to make tea due to their soothing and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to calm the digestive system and reduce heartburn and acid reflux.
Stems: The stems of the Aloe barbadensis plant are used to make tea as they contain anti-inflammatory compounds that help to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate GERD symptoms.
Roots: Aloe vera roots are used to make tea due to their potential to reduce inflammation and soothe the digestive system, which can help to alleviate heartburn and acid reflux associated with GERD.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 10-20 mature leaves of aloe barbadensis by cutting them from the plant with a sharp knife.
Wash the harvested leaves under cold running water for 5 minutes to remove dirt and bacteria.
Peel the leaves using a vegetable peeler and puree them in a blender to create a smooth paste.
Steep 2-3 grams of the aloe paste in 1 liter of boiling water for 10-15 minutes to infuse the liquid.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container to remove the solids.
